title: Turkish lira crashes 15% after central bank governor fired, capital controls feared
topicContext: EUR/USD is affected by emerging market currency crises through multiple channels. European banks have significant exposure to Turkey — Spanish, French, and Italian banks collectively hold over €80 billion in Turkish assets. A Turkish financial crisis raises credit risk concerns for European banks, widens European CDS spreads, and weighs on the euro. The euro also weakens during EM crises because investors shift to dollar-denominated safe havens. However, if the crisis is contained, the impact on EUR/USD is typically modest and short-lived. The lira has lost over 60% of its value against the dollar since 2021, and Turkey's unorthodox monetary policy (cutting rates during high inflation under political pressure) has been a recurring source of market instability.
body:
The Turkish lira plunged 15% against the dollar on Saturday in its sharpest single-day decline since the 2021 currency crisis after President Erdogan abruptly fired the central bank governor and replaced her with a political loyalist who has publicly advocated for lower interest rates despite inflation running above 45%.

The lira fell to 42.50 against the dollar from 37.00 at Friday's close, before partially recovering to 40.80 as the central bank intervened by selling an estimated $5 billion in foreign currency reserves. The move wiped out nearly all the currency's gains since the appointment of a more orthodox policy team in mid-2024.

The newly appointed governor, a former ruling-party parliamentarian with no central banking experience, issued a statement saying the bank would 'pursue policies that support growth and employment' and that 'excessively high interest rates are the cause of inflation, not the cure.' Turkey's policy rate currently stands at 35%, raised from 8.5% over the past two years by the outgoing governor.

Markets reacted with alarm. Turkish five-year credit default swaps jumped 180 basis points to 520, the widest level since 2022. The Istanbul stock exchange fell 7.2% before trading was halted. Turkish dollar-denominated government bonds fell sharply, with the 2030 benchmark dropping 8 cents to 82 cents on the dollar.

The crisis rippled through European markets. Shares of BBVA, the Spanish bank with the largest Turkish exposure through its subsidiary Garanti, fell 6.1%. BNP Paribas and UniCredit, which also have significant Turkish operations, fell 2.8% and 3.4% respectively. The Euro Stoxx Banks index dropped 2.1%.

The euro fell 0.4% against the dollar as investors moved to safe-haven assets, though analysts said the impact on EUR/USD was muted compared to previous Turkish crises because European bank exposure has been gradually reduced. 'The direct contagion channel is smaller than it was in 2018,' said one European economist, 'but the signal is terrible — it tells you that political interference in central banking is alive and well, and that makes investors nervous about the entire EM complex.'

The IMF issued a statement calling for 'transparent and credible monetary policy' and said it was 'ready to engage with Turkish authorities.' The U.S. Treasury Department said it was 'monitoring developments closely.'
